q: A 42-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 180/μL) presents with 6 weeks of productive cough, night sweats, and 15-pound weight loss. His grandmother had similar symptoms years ago and 'took many pills for months.' Chest X-ray shows bilateral upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation. Sputum acid-fast bacilli stain is positive. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
c: A - Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol
e: Active pulmonary tuberculosis requires four-drug therapy with RIPE (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for the initial 2 months, followed by rifampin and isoniazid for 4 additional months (total 6 months). The four-drug regimen is essential to prevent resistance development, especially in HIV-positive patients who have higher rates of drug resistance. Ethambutol is included in the initial phase to cover potential isoniazid resistance until drug susceptibilities are known. Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) should be added to prevent isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy. HIV coinfection requires careful monitoring for drug interactions and immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome, but does not change the standard RIPE regimen for drug-sensitive tuberculosis.
kp: Active tuberculosis requires RIPE therapy for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid for 4 months.
kp_cloze: Active tuberculosis requires [____] therapy for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid for 4 months.

d.Rifampin, isoniazid, and pyrazinamide on: The HIV status and family history might suggest starting with three drugs to reduce pill burden, but omitting ethambutol risks inadequate coverage of potential isoniazid-resistant tuberculosis, which occurs in approximately 10% of cases and would lead to treatment failure.
d.Levofloxacin and azithromycin: The HIV status and respiratory symptoms could suggest atypical pneumonia or MAC infection, making this combination tempting, but the positive acid-fast bacilli stain, upper lobe cavitation, and constitutional symptoms clearly indicate tuberculosis requiring anti-tuberculous therapy.
d.Rifampin and isoniazid only: The grandmother's reference to 'many pills for months' might suggest starting with fewer medications, but two-drug therapy is inadequate for active tuberculosis and carries high risk of developing drug resistance, particularly in immunocompromised patients.

brainstorm.action_content: RIPE therapy for 2 months then RI for 4 months
brainstorm.dx_distractor: atypical pneumonia - HIV makes this tempting but AFB positive rules out
brainstorm.action_distractor: three-drug therapy - seems reasonable but inadequate resistance coverage

phrase: Active TB needs RIPE for 2 months then RI for 4
References: Nahid P, Dorman SE, Alipanah N, et al. Official American Thoracic Society/Centers for Disease Control and Prevention/Infectious Diseases Society of America Clinical Practice Guidelines: Treatment of Drug-Susceptible Tuberculosis. Clin Infect Dis. 2016. PMID: 27516382
Burman WJ, Gallicano K, Peloquin C. Comparative pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of the rifamycin antibacterials. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2001. PMID: 11735609

Sterling TR, Njie G, Zenner D, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection: Recommendations from the National Tuberculosis Controllers Association and CDC. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2020. PMID: 32053584
Subject: Infectious Diseases

q: A 52-year-old executive presents with 2 years of progressive inappropriate social behavior, disinhibition, and new profanity use. His wife notes personality changes and poor judgment at work, though his secretary mentions he still remembers all client names and appointment details perfectly. He recently started collecting staplers compulsively. Neurological examination is normal. Mini-Mental State Examination score is 28/30. What is the most likely diagnosis?
C: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
e: Frontotemporal dementia typically presents in patients aged 45-65 with personality and behavioral changes as the predominant early features, unlike Alzheimer disease which primarily affects memory initially. The patient demonstrates classic behavioral variant FTD findings: disinhibition, inappropriate social conduct, repetitive behaviors (stapler collecting), and personality changes, while memory remains relatively preserved (remembering client details, normal MMSE). The executive dysfunction and loss of social awareness with preserved memory function is pathognomonic for behavioral variant FTD. This condition involves frontotemporal cortical atrophy with tau protein deposits forming Pick bodies. Language deficits may develop later, but behavioral changes dominate the early presentation, making this distinct from other dementias.
kp: Frontotemporal dementia presents with personality changes and preserved memory in patients aged 45-65.
kp_cloze: Frontotemporal dementia presents with [____] changes and preserved memory in patients aged 45-65.

d.Alzheimer disease: The preserved memory function (remembering client names, normal MMSE score) argues against Alzheimer disease, which characteristically presents with progressive memory loss as the earliest and most prominent feature, typically in older patients.
d.Major depressive disorder with psychotic: The behavioral changes and disinhibition could suggest a psychiatric condition, but the progressive nature, compulsive behaviors, and lack of mood symptoms make a neurodegenerative process more likely than primary psychiatric illness.
d.Normal pressure hydrocephalus: The executive dysfunction might suggest NPH, but the patient lacks the classic triad of gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline, and the prominent personality changes are not typical of NPH presentation.

brainstorm.hy_clue: 52yo, personality changes, disinhibition, preserved memory, compulsive behavior
brainstorm.dx_distractor: Alzheimer disease - but memory is preserved
brainstorm.action_distractor: depression workup - but progressive neurodegenerative pattern

phrase: FTD: personality first, memory spared
References: Bang J, Spina S, Miller BL. Frontotemporal dementia. Lancet. 2015. PMID: 26987701
Rascovsky K, Hodges JR, Knopman D, et al. Sensitivity of revised diagnostic criteria for behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia. Brain. 2011. PMID: 21810890
Olney NT, Spina S, Miller BL. Frontotemporal Dementia. Neurol Clin. 2017. PMID: 28410659
Subject: Neurology

q: A 58-year-old woman presents with brief episodes of spinning sensation triggered by rolling over in bed or looking up at shelves. Episodes last 10-30 seconds and resolve spontaneously. She denies hearing loss or tinnitus but reports mild anxiety about driving. Physical examination shows rotatory nystagmus with the left ear down during Dix-Hallpike testing. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
e: This patient has classic benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) of the posterior semicircular canal, confirmed by the positive Dix-Hallpike test with rotatory nystagmus. BPPV results from otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals) displaced into the semicircular canals, causing brief vertigo with position changes. The Epley maneuver (canalith repositioning procedure) is the definitive treatment, with success rates of 80-90% after a single session. The maneuver uses gravity and specific head positions to guide displaced otoconia back to the utricle where they belong. Most patients experience immediate symptom resolution. Medications provide minimal benefit and may delay recovery by suppressing central compensation mechanisms.
kp: Positive Dix-Hallpike test with rotatory nystagmus confirms BPPV requiring Epley maneuver for definitive treatment.
kp_cloze: Positive Dix-Hallpike test with rotatory nystagmus confirms BPPV requiring [____] for definitive treatment.

d.Meclizine 25 mg three times daily: The patient's anxiety about driving might suggest need for symptomatic relief with vestibular suppressants, but meclizine only masks symptoms and may delay natural compensation; the Epley maneuver provides definitive cure by repositioning displaced otoconia.
d.MRI brain with gadolinium: The positional vertigo could raise concern for central causes like vertebrobasilar insufficiency or cerebellar pathology, but the classic brief duration, positive Dix-Hallpike test, and lack of neurological signs confirm peripheral BPPV not requiring imaging.
d.Audiometry and tympanometry: While comprehensive evaluation might seem appropriate given the patient's concern about driving safety, the absence of hearing loss or tinnitus with classic positional symptoms and positive Dix-Hallpike test confirms BPPV, making hearing tests unnecessary before treatment.

brainstorm.hy_clue: Brief positional vertigo, bed rolling trigger, positive Dix-Hallpike, rotatory nystagmus
brainstorm.dx_distractor: Vestibular neuritis - lacks positional trigger specificity
brainstorm.action_distractor: Meclizine - only symptomatic, delays recovery

phrase: Brief positional vertigo with positive Dix-Hallpike needs Epley maneuver
References: Bhattacharyya N, Gubbels SP, Schwartz SR, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2017. PMID: 28248609
Hilton MP, Pinder DK. The Epley (canalith repositioning) manoeuvre for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014. PMID: 25482695
von Brevern M, Bertholon P, Brandt T, et al. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo: Diagnostic criteria. J Vestib Res. 2015. PMID: 26756126
Subject: Otolaryngology

q: A 72-year-old woman presents with severe right-sided headache and jaw pain when eating for 2 weeks. She reports brief episodes of visual blurriness in her right eye. Her daughter is concerned about possible stroke given her age. Physical examination shows tenderness over the right temporal artery. ESR is 89 mm/hr (0-30) and CRP is 45 mg/L (3). What is the most appropriate next step in management?
A: Obtain temporal artery biopsy within 24 hours
C: Order CT angiogram of head and neck
e: This patient presents with classic temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis): age over 50, new severe headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, temporal artery tenderness, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers. Temporal arteritis is an ophthalmologic emergency due to risk of irreversible blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1mg/kg/day or 60-80mg daily) must be started immediately upon clinical suspicion to prevent permanent vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy should be performed for definitive diagnosis but must NOT delay steroid initiation. Biopsy remains diagnostic for up to 2 weeks after steroid initiation due to skip lesions and persistent inflammatory changes. Delaying treatment for diagnostic confirmation risks catastrophic visual complications in up to 20% of untreated patients.
kp: Suspected temporal arteritis requires immediate high-dose prednisone to prevent irreversible blindness before biopsy confirmation.
kp_cloze: Suspected temporal arteritis requires immediate [____] to prevent irreversible blindness before biopsy confirmation.

d.Obtain temporal artery biopsy within 24: The elevated inflammatory markers and temporal artery tenderness support the need for tissue diagnosis, but delaying steroid treatment to obtain biopsy first risks irreversible vision loss; steroids must be started immediately with biopsy performed within 2 weeks.
d.Order CT angiogram of head and neck: The daughter's stroke concern and visual symptoms might suggest large vessel pathology requiring vascular imaging, but the combination of jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, and very high ESR indicates temporal arteritis requiring immediate steroids, not imaging.
d.Schedule ophthalmology consultation: Visual symptoms and age-related concerns warrant ophthalmologic evaluation, but temporal arteritis is a medical emergency requiring immediate corticosteroids; ophthalmology consultation should occur concurrently with steroid initiation, not as the primary next step.

brainstorm.hy_clue: age >70, new severe headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, temporal artery tenderness
brainstorm.dx_distractor: stroke/TIA - visual symptoms and age suggest cerebrovascular, but jaw claudication and temporal tenderness indicate vasculitis
brainstorm.action_distractor: temporal artery biopsy first - seems logical for definitive diagnosis but delays vision-saving treatment

phrase: Temporal arteritis: steroids first, biopsy within 2 weeks, save sight
References: Dasgupta B, Borg FA, Hassan N, et al. BSR and BHPR guidelines for the management of giant cell arteritis. Rheumatology. 2010. PMID: 20488893
Bienvenu B, Ly KH, Lambert M, et al. Management of giant cell arteritis: recommendations of the French Study Group for Large Vessel Vasculitis. Rev Med Interne. 2016. PMID: 27061552
Mackie SL, Dejaco C, Appenzeller S, et al. British Society for Rheumatology guideline on diagnosis and treatment of giant cell arteritis. Rheumatology. 2020. PMID: 32239186
Subject: Internal Medicine

q: A 32-year-old Black woman presents with 6 weeks of dry cough, shortness of breath, and fatigue. She recently moved from Arizona and wonders if she has Valley fever. Physical examination shows bilateral ankle arthritis and tender erythematous nodules on her shins. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: This presentation suggests sarcoidosis with Löfgren syndrome (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthritis). While ACE levels may be elevated in sarcoidosis, they are neither sensitive nor specific and should not be used for diagnosis. Definitive sarcoidosis diagnosis requires tissue confirmation showing non-caseating granulomas plus exclusion of other granulomatous diseases. Transbronchial biopsy has high diagnostic yield for pulmonary sarcoidosis, especially with hilar adenopathy. The recent move from Arizona creates a reasonable concern for coccidioidomycosis, but the classic triad of findings strongly favors sarcoidosis. Biopsy will differentiate between infectious granulomatous diseases and sarcoidosis while providing the tissue confirmation required for definitive diagnosis.
kp: Sarcoidosis diagnosis requires tissue biopsy showing non-caseating granulomas plus exclusion of other causes.
kp_cloze: Sarcoidosis diagnosis requires [____] showing non-caseating granulomas plus exclusion of other causes.
d: Coccidioides serologies: The recent move from Arizona (endemic area) and pulmonary symptoms make coccidioidomycosis a reasonable consideration, but the classic triad of bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthritis (Löfgren syndrome) is pathognomonic for sarcoidosis, requiring tissue confirmation first.

Serum ACE level: While ACE levels are often elevated in sarcoidosis, they are neither sensitive nor specific for diagnosis and can be elevated in other granulomatous diseases; ACE should only be used for monitoring treatment response, not for initial diagnosis.

Tuberculin skin test: The granulomatous presentation could raise concern for tuberculosis, but the symmetric bilateral hilar adenopathy with erythema nodosum and arthritis strongly suggests sarcoidosis rather than TB, which typically shows asymmetric or unilateral involvement.
References: Crouser ED, Maier LA, Wilson KC, et al. Diagnosis and Detection of Sarcoidosis. An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2020. PMID: 32293205
Iannuzzi MC, Rybicki BA, Teirstein AS. Sarcoidosis. N Engl J Med. 2007. PMID: 18032765
Hunninghake GW, Costabel U, Ando M, et al. ATS/ERS/WASOG statement on sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis Vasc Diffuse Lung Dis. 1999. PMID: 10546120
Subject: Internal Medicine

brainstorm.dx_distractor: coccidioidomycosis due to Arizona residence + respiratory symptoms, but lacks typical unilateral findings
brainstorm.action_distractor: ACE level seems logical for sarcoidosis workup, but not diagnostic and should only monitor treatment

phrase: ACE levels monitor sarcoidosis treatment, never diagnose it

q: A 22-year-old man presents with 8 hours of abdominal pain that began around his umbilicus and now localizes to the right lower quadrant. He mentions eating gas station sushi yesterday and wonders if he has food poisoning. He has nausea, decreased appetite, and low-grade fever. Examination shows tenderness at McBurney point with positive Rovsing sign. WBC 13,200/uL (4,500-11,000). What is the most appropriate next step in management?
A: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
B: Stool culture and ova and parasites
c: A - CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
e: This patient presents with classic acute appendicitis: periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant, anorexia, nausea, low-grade fever, leukocytosis, and positive McBurney point tenderness with Rovsing sign. In adults with suspected appendicitis, CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast is the imaging modality of choice, providing 95% sensitivity and specificity for diagnosis while evaluating for complications like perforation or abscess formation. The history of questionable food consumption is a red herring - the clinical presentation and physical exam findings strongly suggest appendicitis rather than infectious gastroenteritis. CT imaging will confirm the diagnosis and guide surgical planning for laparoscopic appendectomy.
kp: Adult appendicitis requires CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast for diagnosis before surgical intervention.
kp_cloze: Adult appendicitis requires [____] for diagnosis before surgical intervention.

d.Stool culture and ova and parasites: The gas station sushi history creates suspicion for foodborne illness, but the classic pain migration pattern, McBurney point tenderness, Rovsing sign, and leukocytosis indicate appendicitis rather than infectious gastroenteritis, which typically presents with diarrhea and diffuse abdominal cramping.
d.Upper endoscopy: Nausea and food history might suggest upper GI pathology, but the right lower quadrant localization with positive appendicitis signs and absence of upper abdominal symptoms make appendicitis far more likely than peptic ulcer disease or gastritis.
d.Empiric ciprofloxacin and metronidazole: While these antibiotics treat intra-abdominal infections, starting empiric treatment without imaging confirmation would delay necessary surgical intervention and risk complications from untreated appendicitis, including perforation and abscess formation.

brainstorm.action_content: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast in adult
brainstorm.dx_distractor: gastroenteritis from bad sushi - lacks diarrhea, has focal RLQ signs
brainstorm.action_distractor: empiric antibiotics - delays surgery, risks perforation

phrase: Periumbilical to RLQ migration with McBurney tenderness equals appendicitis
References: Di Saverio S, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of acute appendicitis: 2020 update of the WSES Jerusalem guidelines. World J Emerg Surg. 2020. PMID: 32295644
Kalisya LM, et al. Validation of the Adult Appendicitis Score for adults with suspected appendicitis. World J Emerg Surg. 2020. PMID: 32293680
Monsonis Centelles S, et al. The role of imaging in acute appendicitis. Insights Imaging. 2021. PMID: 33411058
Subject: Surgery

q: A 28-year-old teacher presents with 6 months of episodic shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough that wake her at 3 AM twice weekly. Her grandmother had emphysema from smoking. Physical examination shows mild expiratory wheeze. Chest X-ray is normal. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: This patient presents with classic asthma symptoms: episodic dyspnea, nocturnal cough, and wheeze. Spirometry with bronchodilator response is the gold standard initial pulmonary function test for asthma diagnosis. A positive test shows FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 with ≥12% and ≥200mL improvement in FEV1 after bronchodilator administration. This reversible airway obstruction pattern confirms asthma. The nocturnal symptoms and episodic nature strongly suggest asthma rather than COPD. Methacholine challenge testing is reserved for patients with high clinical suspicion but normal spirometry, as it has high sensitivity but lower specificity. The family history of emphysema serves as a clinical red herring, as this patient's young age, symptom pattern, and lack of smoking history point toward asthma rather than inherited alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.
kp: Asthma diagnosis requires spirometry showing reversible obstruction: ≥12% and 200mL FEV1 improvement post-bronchodilator.
kp_cloze: Asthma diagnosis requires spirometry showing reversible obstruction: ≥[____]% and 200mL FEV1 improvement post-bronchodilator.

d.Methacholine challenge test: While methacholine challenge has high sensitivity for asthma, it should only be performed when spirometry is normal despite high clinical suspicion; this patient's wheeze on examination suggests likely abnormal spirometry, making bronchodilator response testing the appropriate first step.
d.High-resolution CT chest: The family history of emphysema might suggest structural lung disease requiring detailed imaging, but this young nonsmoker with episodic nocturnal symptoms and wheeze has a classic asthma presentation that requires functional assessment, not anatomical imaging.
d.Peak expiratory flow monitoring: PEF monitoring can support asthma diagnosis through variability documentation, but it cannot establish the initial diagnosis; spirometry with bronchodilator response provides the definitive reversible obstruction criteria needed for asthma confirmation.

brainstorm.action_content: spirometry with bronchodilator response for reversible obstruction
brainstorm.dx_distractor: COPD from alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency + young age and episodic pattern inconsistent
brainstorm.action_distractor: methacholine challenge + should do spirometry first before provocation testing

phrase: Nocturnal cough plus wheeze equals spirometry with bronchodilator first
References: Global Initiative for Asthma. Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2023. PMID: 36198424
Aaron SD, Vandemheen KL, FitzGerald JM, et al. Reevaluation of Diagnosis in Adults With Physician-Diagnosed Asthma. JAMA. 2017. PMID: 28114551
Pellegrino R, Viegi G, Brusasco V, et al. Interpretative Strategies for Lung Function Tests. Eur Respir J. 2005. PMID: 16264058
Subject: Internal Medicine

q: A 58-year-old man undergoes screening colonoscopy. A 12mm tubular adenoma with high-grade dysplasia is removed from the sigmoid colon. He has a family history of breast cancer in his sister. Pathology shows complete excision with clear margins. He asks about his cancer risk and follow-up needs. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
D: Genetic counseling for hereditary cancer syndromes
e: This patient has an advanced adenoma based on size ≥10mm and high-grade dysplasia. Advanced adenomas are defined as adenomas ≥10mm, containing villous features, or having high-grade dysplasia. These lesions carry higher malignant potential and require more intensive surveillance. Current guidelines recommend repeat colonoscopy in 3 years for advanced adenomas after complete removal. The 12mm size and high-grade dysplasia both qualify this as advanced, regardless of histologic subtype. Family history of breast cancer alone does not indicate hereditary colorectal cancer syndromes. Complete excision with clear margins indicates successful polypectomy without immediate concern for residual tissue.
kp: Advanced adenomas (≥10mm, villous features, or high-grade dysplasia) require repeat colonoscopy in 3 years
kp_cloze: Advanced adenomas (≥10mm, villous features, or high-grade dysplasia) require repeat colonoscopy in [____] years

d.Repeat colonoscopy in 1 year: The mention of multiple risk factors (size, dysplasia, family history) might suggest very high-risk requiring annual surveillance, but 1-year follow-up is reserved for patients with >10 adenomas or inadequate bowel preparation, not single advanced adenomas.
d.Repeat colonoscopy in 5 years: Since this is a tubular adenoma which typically follows the 5-7 year interval for small adenomas, one might default to standard screening intervals, but the high-grade dysplasia and ≥10mm size make this an advanced adenoma requiring shorter 3-year surveillance.
d.Genetic counseling for hereditary cancer: The family history of breast cancer could suggest hereditary cancer syndromes like Lynch syndrome, but isolated breast cancer in one relative without early onset colorectal or endometrial cancers does not meet criteria for genetic evaluation.

References: Gupta S, Lieberman D, Anderson JC, et al. Recommendations for Follow-Up After Colonoscopy and Polypectomy: A Consensus Update. Gastroenterology. 2020. PMID: 32044092
Hassan C, Quintero E, Dumonceau JM, et al. Post-polypectomy Colonoscopy Surveillance: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy Guidelines. Endoscopy. 2020. PMID: 32023820
Rex DK, Boland CR, Dominitz JA, et al. Colorectal Cancer Screening: Recommendations for Physicians and Patients from the U.S. Multi-Society Task Force. Gastroenterology. 2017. PMID: 28600072
Subject: Gastroenterology

brainstorm.action_content: repeat colonoscopy in 3 years for advanced adenoma
brainstorm.dx_distractor: hereditary cancer syndrome - family history suggests but criteria not met
brainstorm.action_distractor: 5-year surveillance - appropriate for small adenomas but not advanced lesions

phrase: Advanced adenoma: big, bad, or ugly means 3-year surveillance

q: A 45-year-old violinist presents with 8 months of progressive right-sided hearing loss and tinnitus. She reports occasional dizziness during orchestra performances and attributes symptoms to loud rehearsals. Audiometry shows unilateral right sensorineural hearing loss. Weber test lateralizes to the left ear. Family history reveals a sister with bilateral hearing aids since age 30. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
D: Occupational hearing assessment with noise exposure history
e: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus and vestibular symptoms raises concern for retrocochlear pathology, specifically acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma). The family history of early bilateral hearing loss suggests possible neurofibromatosis type 2, which is associated with bilateral acoustic neuromas. MRI with gadolinium is the gold standard for diagnosing acoustic neuromas, providing excellent soft tissue contrast to visualize the cerebellopontine angle and internal auditory canal. These tumors arise from Schwann cells of the vestibular nerve and typically present with the classic triad of unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and imbalance. Early diagnosis is crucial as larger tumors may cause facial nerve weakness, brainstem compression, or hydrocephalus.
kp: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss requires MRI with gadolinium to rule out acoustic neuroma.
kp_cloze: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss requires [____] to rule out acoustic neuroma.

d.CT scan of temporal bones: The progressive hearing loss and tinnitus could suggest middle ear pathology or temporal bone abnormalities, but CT lacks the soft tissue resolution needed to visualize acoustic neuromas in the cerebellopontine angle and internal auditory canal where these tumors characteristically occur.
d.Tympanometry and acoustic reflexes: The hearing loss symptoms might prompt evaluation of middle ear function, but the sensorineural pattern on audiometry with Weber lateralization indicates inner ear or retrocochlear pathology that tympanometry cannot assess.
d.Occupational hearing assessment with noi: The patient's profession as a violinist and attribution of symptoms to loud rehearsals suggests noise-induced hearing loss, but bilateral symmetric high-frequency loss would be expected rather than unilateral sensorineural hearing loss with vestibular symptoms.

brainstorm.action_content: MRI with gadolinium for retrocochlear pathology
brainstorm.dx_distractor: noise-induced hearing loss - unilateral presentation atypical
brainstorm.action_distractor: occupational assessment - ignores retrocochlear red flags

phrase: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss = acoustic neuroma until proven otherwise
References: Carlson ML, Link MJ. Vestibular Schwannoma. N Engl J Med. 2021. PMID: 34379917
Marinelli JP, Lohse CM, Carlson ML. Incidence of Vestibular Schwannoma over the Past Half-Century. Otol Neurotol. 2018. PMID: 29664853
Plotkin SR, Blakeley JO, Evans DG, et al. Update from the 2011 International Schwannomatosis Workshop. Am J Med Genet A. 2013. PMID: 23152427
Subject: Otolaryngology

q: A 52-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis presents with increasing abdominal girth and confusion over 2 weeks. His wife mentions recent upper respiratory symptoms treated with antibiotics. Physical examination shows moderate ascites and asterixis. Laboratory results: albumin 2.8 g/dL (3.5-5.0), bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL (0.3-1.2), INR 1.8 (0.8-1.1), creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (0.7-1.3). What is the most appropriate next step in management?
A: Calculate MELD score and refer for transplant evaluation
c: A - Calculate MELD score and refer for transplant evaluation
e: This patient presents with decompensated cirrhosis evidenced by new-onset ascites and hepatic encephalopathy (asterixis, confusion). The MELD score calculation uses bilirubin (3.2), INR (1.8), and creatinine (1.4), yielding approximately 19 points. MELD scores ≥15 indicate significant mortality risk and warrant transplant evaluation. The Child-Pugh score would be class C (decompensated) given low albumin, elevated bilirubin, prolonged INR, moderate ascites, and grade 2 encephalopathy. While symptomatic management with lactulose is appropriate, the priority is transplant evaluation given the high MELD score and decompensated state. Recent infection may have precipitated decompensation, but the underlying severity necessitates definitive evaluation.
kp: MELD score ≥15 with decompensated cirrhosis requires transplant evaluation as priority management.
kp_cloze: MELD score ≥[____] with decompensated cirrhosis requires transplant evaluation as priority management.

d.Start lactulose and rifaximin: The asterixis and confusion suggest hepatic encephalopathy requiring symptomatic treatment, making this seem appropriate, but with MELD score ≥15 and clear decompensation, transplant evaluation takes priority over symptomatic management alone.
d.Perform diagnostic paracentesis: New ascites in a cirrhotic patient could indicate spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, especially with recent respiratory symptoms, but the combination of high MELD score and decompensated state makes transplant evaluation the priority over ruling out SBP.
d.Order CT abdomen with contrast: The increasing abdominal girth might suggest complications like hepatocellular carcinoma requiring imaging, but contrast is contraindicated with elevated creatinine, and transplant evaluation addresses both the underlying severity and need for HCC screening.

References: Kamath PS, Wiesner RH, Malinchoc M, et al. A model to predict survival in patients with end-stage liver disease. Hepatology. 2001. PMID: 11230732
Martin P, DiMartini A, Feng S, et al. Evaluation for liver transplantation in adults: 2013 practice guideline. Hepatology. 2014. PMID: 24399440
Pugh RN, Murray-Lyon IM, Dawson JL, et al. Transection of the oesophagus for bleeding oesophageal varices. Br J Surg. 1973. PMID: 4541083
Subject: Gastroenterology

brainstorm.hy_clue: Decompensated cirrhosis, ascites, encephalopathy, high MELD components, recent infection trigger
brainstorm.action_content: Calculate MELD score and refer for transplant evaluation
brainstorm.dx_distractor: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis - ascites + infection history suggests SBP but MELD severity takes priority
brainstorm.action_distractor: Symptomatic encephalopathy treatment - asterixis suggests immediate lactulose need but transplant evaluation more urgent

phrase: MELD ≥15 trumps symptomatic management - transplant evaluation first

q: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of fatigue and diffuse rash covering her trunk, palms, and soles. She reports unprotected intercourse 6 weeks ago and mentions a small genital ulcer that resolved spontaneously 3 weeks ago. She has been taking amoxicillin for presumed strep throat without improvement. Physical examination reveals non-tender lymphadenopathy and copper-colored maculopapular lesions on palms and soles. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: This patient presents with classic secondary syphilis: painless primary chancre that resolved, followed by systemic symptoms and the pathognomonic diffuse maculopapular rash involving palms and soles. The non-response to amoxicillin and non-tender lymphadenopathy further support syphilis. RPR (rapid plasma reagin) is the appropriate initial screening test for syphilis. It is a non-treponemal test that becomes positive in secondary syphilis and provides quantitative titers useful for monitoring treatment response. If positive, confirmation with treponemal-specific testing (FTA-ABS or TP-PA) follows. The timing (6 weeks post-exposure) and clinical presentation are classic for secondary syphilis, which occurs 4-10 weeks after initial infection when spirochetes disseminate systemically.
kp: Secondary syphilis presents with diffuse maculopapular rash including palms and soles; screen with RPR.
kp_cloze: Secondary syphilis presents with diffuse maculopapular rash including [____]; screen with RPR.
d: Blood cultures: The fatigue, lymphadenopathy, and systemic symptoms could suggest bacteremia, but the characteristic palm and sole involvement with copper-colored lesions and history of resolved genital ulcer are pathognomonic for secondary syphilis, not bacterial sepsis.

Skin biopsy: The diffuse nature of the rash might prompt consideration of a dermatologic condition requiring tissue diagnosis, but the classic distribution involving palms and soles combined with the antecedent painless ulcer makes this unnecessary when RPR can provide the diagnosis.

Monospot test: The fatigue, lymphadenopathy, and sore throat history could suggest infectious mononucleosis, but the palm and sole involvement is not seen in EBV and the resolved genital ulcer points specifically toward syphilis rather than viral illness.

brainstorm.dx_distractor: infectious mononucleosis + lacks palm/sole involvement
brainstorm.action_distractor: skin biopsy + serologic testing more specific

phrase: Palms and soles rash = secondary syphilis until proven otherwise
References: Workowski KA, Bolan GA, CDC. Sexually Transmitted Diseases Treatment Guidelines, 2015. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2015. PMID: 26042815
Peeling RW, Mabey D, Kamb ML, et al. Syphilis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017. PMID: 29022569
Tuddenham S, Ghanem KG. Emerging trends and persistent challenges in the management of adult syphilis. BMC Infect Dis. 2018. PMID: 30419848
Subject: Internal Medicine

q: A 29-year-old graduate student presents with 8 months of persistent worry about multiple daily concerns including finances, work performance, and relationships. She reports restlessness, muscle tension, and difficulty concentrating on her thesis. Her roommate mentions she seems constantly "on edge" and has trouble sleeping. She denies panic attacks but worries this anxiety means she's "losing her mind." What is the most likely diagnosis?
B: Major depressive disorder with anxious distress
e: This patient meets criteria for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD): excessive worry about multiple life domains lasting over 6 months, accompanied by at least 3 associated symptoms (restlessness, muscle tension, concentration difficulty, sleep disturbance). GAD involves pervasive, difficult-to-control worry that is disproportionate to actual circumstances and causes functional impairment. The absence of discrete panic episodes and the chronic, persistent nature of multi-domain worry distinguish GAD from panic disorder. The duration exceeds 6 months and predates any clear stressor, making adjustment disorder unlikely. While anxiety can accompany depression, the primary presentation focuses on worry rather than mood symptoms, making GAD the most appropriate diagnosis.
kp: GAD requires excessive worry over 6 months plus 3+ symptoms: restlessness, fatigue, concentration issues, irritability, muscle tension, sleep problems.
kp_cloze: GAD requires excessive worry over [____] plus 3+ symptoms: restlessness, fatigue, concentration issues, irritability, muscle tension, sleep problems.

d.Major depressive disorder with anxious d: The concentration difficulty and sleep problems could suggest depression with anxiety features, but the primary complaint is persistent worry across multiple domains rather than depressed mood, anhedonia, or other core depressive symptoms that would be prominent in MDD.
d.Adjustment disorder with anxiety: The chronic worry about finances and work performance might appear reactive to graduate school stressors, but adjustment disorder requires symptom onset within 6 months of an identifiable stressor, while this patient has 8 months of symptoms without a clear precipitating event.
d.Panic disorder: The patient's fear about 'losing her mind' and being constantly 'on edge' could suggest panic-related anxiety, but she explicitly denies panic attacks, and her presentation involves chronic generalized worry rather than discrete episodes of intense fear with physical symptoms.

brainstorm.hy_clue: 8 months persistent worry, restlessness, muscle tension, concentration difficulty, sleep trouble
brainstorm.action_content: diagnose GAD based on duration and symptom criteria
brainstorm.dx_distractor: adjustment disorder - seems reactive to school stress but exceeds timeframe
brainstorm.action_distractor: attributing to normal school stress - minimizes pathological worry pattern

phrase: GAD: 6+ months excessive worry plus 3 physical/cognitive symptoms
References: Stein MB, Sareen J. Generalized Anxiety Disorder. N Engl J Med. 2015. PMID: 26465987
Bandelow B, Michaelis S, Wedekind D. Treatment of anxiety disorders. Dialogues Clin Neurosci. 2017. PMID: 28179809
Kessler RC, Chiu WT, Demler O, et al. Prevalence, severity, and comorbidity of 12-month DSM-IV disorders. Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2005. PMID: 15939837
Subject: Psychiatry

q: A 28-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis presents for disease-modifying therapy selection. She has had 3 relapses in the past 2 years with incomplete recovery. Her uncle died from progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy while on immunosuppression for lupus. MRI shows 8 new T2 lesions since diagnosis 18 months ago. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
D: Observation with repeat MRI in 6 months
e: This patient has highly active relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis based on frequent relapses with incomplete recovery and significant radiological progression (8 new lesions). High-efficacy disease-modifying therapy is indicated. While natalizumab offers excellent efficacy, the family history of PML creates patient concern and relative contraindication despite low absolute risk. Ocrelizumab, a CD20 monoclonal antibody, provides high efficacy comparable to natalizumab without PML risk. It reduces relapse rates by 46-47% and disability progression by 40% compared to interferon. Interferon-beta and glatiramer acetate are considered low-efficacy options inappropriate for highly active disease. The substantial radiological and clinical activity precludes observation, as untreated highly active MS leads to irreversible disability accumulation.
kp: Highly active relapsing-remitting MS requires high-efficacy DMT; ocrelizumab offers excellent efficacy without PML risk.
kp_cloze: Highly active relapsing-remitting MS requires [____] DMT; ocrelizumab offers excellent efficacy without PML risk.

d.Interferon-beta: The multiple relapses and radiological progression suggest highly active disease requiring aggressive treatment, but interferon-beta is a low-efficacy first-line agent with only 30% relapse rate reduction, insufficient for this patient's disease activity level.
d.Natalizumab: While natalizumab would be appropriate for highly active disease with excellent efficacy, the family history of PML in the uncle creates legitimate patient concern and relative contraindication, making ocrelizumab a safer high-efficacy alternative.
d.Observation with repeat MRI in 6 months: The combination of frequent relapses with incomplete recovery and 8 new T2 lesions demonstrates highly active disease requiring immediate intervention, as delayed treatment in active MS leads to irreversible disability accumulation.

brainstorm.hy_clue: RRMS, 3 relapses/2 years, incomplete recovery, 8 new T2 lesions, family PML history
brainstorm.dx_distractor: stable RRMS requiring low-efficacy DMT - fails due to high activity markers
brainstorm.action_distractor: natalizumab despite PML family history - fails due to patient safety concerns

phrase: Highly active RRMS needs high-efficacy DMT - ocrelizumab without PML risk
References: Hauser SL, Bar-Or A, Comi G, et al. Ocrelizumab versus Interferon Beta-1a in Relapsing Multiple Sclerosis. N Engl J Med. 2017. PMID: 28002679
Polman CH, O'Connor PW, Havrdova E, et al. A randomized, placebo-controlled trial of natalizumab for relapsing multiple sclerosis. N Engl J Med. 2006. PMID: 16481890
Rae-Grant A, Day GS, Marrie RA, et al. Practice guideline recommendations summary: Disease-modifying therapies for adults with multiple sclerosis. Neurology. 2018. PMID: 29686116
Subject: Neurology

q: A 32-year-old woman at 30 weeks gestation with gestational diabetes presents for routine follow-up. She has been following medical nutrition therapy and exercise for 2 weeks. Her family history includes type 2 diabetes. Fasting glucose is 98 mg/dL and 2-hour postprandial glucose is 135 mg/dL. BMI is 31 kg/m². What is the most appropriate next step in management?
A: Continue current lifestyle modifications and recheck in 2 weeks
e: This patient with gestational diabetes has inadequate glycemic control despite 2 weeks of medical nutrition therapy and exercise, with postprandial glucose of 135 mg/dL exceeding the target of 120 mg/dL. ACOG guidelines recommend insulin as first-line pharmacotherapy when lifestyle modifications fail to achieve glycemic targets (fasting glucose <95 mg/dL, 2-hour postprandial <120 mg/dL). While metformin and glyburide cross the placenta and have been used in pregnancy, insulin remains preferred due to its inability to cross the placental barrier and established safety profile. Maternal hyperglycemia increases risks of macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycemia, and future maternal diabetes. Prompt insulin initiation optimizes maternal and fetal outcomes.
kp: Gestational diabetes with postprandial glucose >120 mg/dL despite lifestyle modifications requires insulin therapy.
kp_cloze: Gestational diabetes with postprandial glucose >120 mg/dL despite lifestyle modifications requires [____] therapy.

d.Continue current lifestyle modifications: The elevated BMI and family history of diabetes might suggest more time is needed for lifestyle changes to take effect, but with postprandial glucose clearly above target (135 vs 120 mg/dL goal), delaying pharmacotherapy risks maternal and fetal complications from ongoing hyperglycemia.
d.Start metformin: The patient's elevated BMI and insulin resistance might make metformin seem appropriate as it addresses these underlying issues, but ACOG guidelines prefer insulin as first-line pharmacotherapy since metformin crosses the placenta while insulin does not.
d.Start glyburide: The family history of type 2 diabetes and current hyperglycemia might suggest glyburide as an oral alternative to avoid injections, but like metformin, glyburide crosses the placenta and insulin remains the preferred first-line agent per current guidelines.

brainstorm.diagnosis: gestational diabetes with inadequate glycemic control
brainstorm.dx_distractor: insulin resistance requiring metformin + fails because insulin preferred first-line
brainstorm.action_distractor: continue lifestyle modifications + fails because glucose targets not met

phrase: Gestational diabetes: postprandial >120 despite lifestyle equals insulin time
References: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 190: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus. Obstet Gynecol. 2018. PMID: 29370047
Landon MB, Spong CY, Thom E, et al. A multicenter, randomized trial of treatment for mild gestational diabetes. N Engl J Med. 2009. PMID: 19797280
Rowan JA, Hague WM, Gao W, et al. Metformin versus insulin for the treatment of gestational diabetes. N Engl J Med. 2008. PMID: 18463375
Subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology

q: A 45-year-old diabetic man presents with 6 weeks of deep, constant left ankle pain and swelling following a minor puncture wound from a rusty nail. He initially improved with oral antibiotics but symptoms returned. Temperature is 99.2°F, and there is warmth and tenderness over the medial malleolus. His wife is concerned about possible septic arthritis. WBC 9,800/uL (4,500-11,000), ESR 78 mm/hr (<20), CRP 45 mg/L (<3). X-rays show subtle lucency in the distal tibia. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: MRI is the most sensitive and specific imaging modality for osteomyelitis diagnosis, with 90-95% sensitivity and specificity. It can detect bone marrow edema, cortical destruction, and soft tissue involvement weeks before changes appear on plain radiographs. In this diabetic patient with penetrating trauma, chronic deep bone pain, elevated inflammatory markers, and subtle radiographic changes, osteomyelitis is highly suspected. MRI will definitively establish the diagnosis, determine extent of infection, and guide surgical planning if needed. The elevated ESR and CRP support an infectious process. While bone biopsy with culture remains the gold standard for organism identification, MRI is the optimal initial imaging test to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding to more invasive procedures.
kp: MRI is the most sensitive and specific imaging modality for diagnosing osteomyelitis.
kp_cloze: [____] is the most sensitive and specific imaging modality for diagnosing osteomyelitis.

d.CT scan of the ankle: The subtle lucency on X-ray might suggest need for better bone detail visualization, but CT has lower sensitivity than MRI for early osteomyelitis and cannot detect bone marrow edema, which is the earliest sign of infection.
d.Bone scan with technetium-99m: The elevated inflammatory markers and bone pain could indicate need for a sensitive screening test, but bone scans have poor specificity and cannot distinguish osteomyelitis from other inflammatory conditions or trauma.
d.Arthrocentesis of the ankle joint: The wife's concern about septic arthritis and the joint swelling might suggest synovial fluid analysis, but the deep constant pain, penetrating trauma history, and radiographic bone changes point to osteomyelitis rather than isolated septic arthritis.

brainstorm.dx_distractor: septic arthritis - lacks joint effusion signs
brainstorm.action_distractor: arthrocentesis - targets wrong anatomical site

phrase: MRI beats all imaging for osteomyelitis diagnosis
References: Pineda C, Vargas A, Rodríguez AV. Imaging of osteomyelitis: current concepts. Infect Dis Clin North Am. 2006. PMID: 16984867
Hatzenbuehler J, Pulling TJ. Diagnosis and management of osteomyelitis. Am Fam Physician. 2011. PMID: 22010614

Berbari EF, Kanj SS, Kowalski TJ. Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis in Adults. Clin Infect Dis. 2015. PMID: 26229122
Subject: Infectious Disease

q: A 58-year-old man with hepatitis C cirrhosis and refractory ascites presents with decreased urine output and altered mental status. His wife reports he stopped taking his spironolactone 5 days ago due to nausea. Examination shows jaundice, ascites, and asterixis. Creatinine has increased from 1.2 mg/dL to 2.6 mg/dL over 10 days. Urinalysis shows no proteinuria or casts. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
e: This patient presents with type 1 hepatorenal syndrome, characterized by rapid deterioration in renal function (doubling of creatinine in <2 weeks) in a patient with advanced liver disease and ascites. The absence of proteinuria, casts, or other causes of acute kidney injury in the setting of cirrhosis supports this diagnosis. Type 1 hepatorenal syndrome is a functional renal impairment caused by severe vasoconstriction of renal arteries due to splanchnic vasodilation and activation of vasoconstrictor systems. The combination of albumin (volume expansion) with midodrine (alpha-1 agonist) and octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstrictor) addresses the underlying pathophysiology by improving effective circulating volume and renal perfusion. This treatment serves as a bridge to liver transplantation, which is the definitive therapy.
kp: Type 1 hepatorenal syndrome requires albumin plus midodrine and octreotide as bridge to transplant
kp_cloze: Type 1 hepatorenal syndrome requires [____] plus midodrine and octreotide as bridge to transplant

d.Immediate hemodialysis: The rapid creatinine rise and oliguria might suggest need for urgent dialysis, but hepatorenal syndrome is a functional disorder that responds to medical therapy targeting the underlying pathophysiology; dialysis doesn't address splanchnic vasodilation and may worsen hemodynamic status in these patients.
d.High-dose furosemide: The fluid retention and ascites could suggest aggressive diuresis is needed, but hepatorenal syndrome results from effective volume depletion despite total body fluid overload; further diuresis would worsen renal hypoperfusion and accelerate kidney injury progression.
d.Restart spironolactone with lactulose: The temporal relationship with stopping spironolactone and the asterixis might suggest hepatic encephalopathy requiring diuretic resumption, but the rapid creatinine doubling indicates type 1 hepatorenal syndrome requiring specific vasopressor therapy, not routine cirrhosis management.

brainstorm.hy_clue: Cirrhosis, rapid creatinine doubling, ascites, no urinalysis abnormalities, altered mental status
brainstorm.dx_distractor: acute tubular necrosis - but lacks casts/proteinuria typical of ATN
brainstorm.action_distractor: hemodialysis - functional disorder needs medical therapy first

phrase: Type 1 hepatorenal: albumin, midodrine, octreotide bridge to transplant
References: Angeli P, Gines P, Wong F, et al. Diagnosis and management of acute kidney injury in patients with cirrhosis. J Hepatol. 2015. PMID: 25800947
Salerno F, Gerbes A, Gines P, et al. Diagnosis, prevention and treatment of hepatorenal syndrome in cirrhosis. Gut. 2007. PMID: 17895356

Wong F, O'Leary JG, Reddy KR, et al. New consensus definition of acute kidney injury accurately predicts 30-day mortality in patients with cirrhosis and infection. Gastroenterology. 2013. PMID: 23958540
Subject: Internal Medicine

q: A 67-year-old diabetic man presents to the ED with severe epigastric pain radiating to his back, fever to 102.8°F, and yellowing of his eyes. His wife mentions he had similar but milder episodes after fatty meals in recent months. Physical examination shows scleral icterus, RUQ tenderness, and altered mental status with confusion about the date. Vital signs show BP 85/50 mmHg, HR 118/min. Total bilirubin 4.2 mg/dL (0.3-1.2). What is the most appropriate initial management?
D: IV fluids and pain control with reassessment
c: B - IV antibiotics and emergent ERCP
e: This patient presents with Reynolds pentad (Charcot triad plus altered mental status and hypotension), indicating severe ascending cholangitis with sepsis. The combination of biliary obstruction signs (jaundice, RUQ pain) with systemic toxicity (fever, hypotension, confusion) represents a surgical emergency requiring immediate biliary decompression. ERCP with sphincterotomy and stent placement is the preferred method for emergent biliary drainage, as it directly addresses the underlying obstruction causing bacterial stasis and translocation. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics targeting gram-negative and anaerobic organisms must be initiated immediately but are insufficient alone. The history of fatty food intolerance suggests choledocholithiasis as the underlying cause. Delay in biliary decompression leads to progressive sepsis and multiorgan failure.
kp: Reynolds pentad requires immediate IV antibiotics plus emergent ERCP for biliary decompression.
kp_cloze: Reynolds pentad requires immediate IV antibiotics plus emergent [____] for biliary decompression.

d.Emergency laparoscopic cholecystectomy: The RUQ tenderness and history of fatty food intolerance might suggest acute cholecystitis requiring surgical intervention, but the presence of jaundice with altered mental status and hypotension indicates ascending cholangitis with biliary obstruction, where drainage takes priority over cholecystectomy.
d.Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placem: The RUQ tenderness and diabetic status might suggest severe cholecystitis warranting percutaneous drainage, but the jaundice and systemic toxicity indicate common bile duct obstruction requiring ERCP, not gallbladder decompression.
d.IV fluids and pain control with reassess: The abdominal pain and hypotension might suggest conservative management with supportive care initially, but Reynolds pentad represents a surgical emergency where delayed biliary decompression leads to irreversible septic shock and death.

brainstorm.hy_clue: RUQ pain + fever + jaundice + AMS + hypotension
brainstorm.action_content: IV antibiotics and emergent ERCP with biliary decompression
brainstorm.dx_distractor: acute cholecystitis + fails due to jaundice indicating CBD obstruction
brainstorm.action_distractor: cholecystectomy + fails because drainage before definitive surgery

phrase: Reynolds pentad equals immediate ERCP - drainage saves lives
References: Kiriyama S, Kozaka K, Takada T, et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholangitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018. PMID: 29032636
Manes G, Paspatis G, Aabakken L, et al. Endoscopic management of common bile duct stones: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy guideline. Endoscopy. 2019. PMID: 30943497
Williams EJ, Green J, Beckingham I, et al. Guidelines on the management of common bile duct stones. Gut. 2008. PMID: 17449424
Subject: Surgery

q: A 48-year-old man presents with fatigue and muscle cramps. His wife reports he has had three episodes of nephrolithiasis over the past year. Blood pressure is 164/98 mmHg on no medications. He denies chest pain but mentions occasional palpitations. Laboratory studies show potassium 2.9 mEq/L (3.5-5.0), bicarbonate 31 mEq/L (22-28), and creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (0.7-1.3). What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: This patient presents with resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and recurrent nephrolithiasis, suggesting primary hyperaldosteronism. The aldosterone-to-renin ratio is the recommended initial screening test for Conn syndrome. A ratio greater than 30 with aldosterone levels above 15 ng/dL suggests primary hyperaldosteronism. The hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis result from excessive mineralocorticoid activity causing potassium and hydrogen ion losses. Nephrolithiasis occurs due to hypocitraturia and hypercalciuria from chronic hypokalemia. After positive screening, confirmatory testing with oral sodium loading or saline infusion suppression test is performed, followed by adrenal imaging to determine if the source is unilateral adenoma or bilateral hyperplasia.
kp: Primary hyperaldosteronism screening requires aldosterone-to-renin ratio as initial diagnostic test.
kp_cloze: Primary hyperaldosteronism screening requires [____] as initial diagnostic test.

d.24-hour urine aldosterone: The recurrent nephrolithiasis might suggest measuring urinary aldosterone, but 24-hour urine aldosterone alone cannot distinguish primary from secondary hyperaldosteronism since both conditions can have elevated aldosterone; the aldosterone-to-renin ratio is required to differentiate these conditions.
d.CT abdomen with contrast: The combination of hypertension and nephrolithiasis could suggest adrenal pathology requiring imaging, but adrenal CT should only be performed after biochemical confirmation of primary hyperaldosteronism with aldosterone-to-renin ratio and confirmatory testing to avoid detecting incidental adrenal masses.
d.Renal ultrasound: The recurrent kidney stones and elevated creatinine might suggest primary renal pathology requiring ultrasound evaluation, but the combination of resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis points to mineralocorticoid excess rather than intrinsic renal disease.

brainstorm.dx_distractor: renal artery stenosis + lacks bruit/asymmetric kidneys
brainstorm.action_distractor: CT imaging first + biochemical confirmation needed first

phrase: Resistant hypertension plus hypokalemia equals aldosterone-to-renin ratio first
References: Funder JW, Carey RM, Mantero F, et al. The Management of Primary Aldosteronism: Case Detection, Diagnosis, and Treatment. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016. PMID: 26934393
Rossi GP, Bernini G, Caliumi C, et al. A Prospective Study of the Prevalence of Primary Aldosteronism in 1,125 Hypertensive Patients. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2006. PMID: 16904534
Young WF, Stanson AW, Thompson GB, et al. Role for Adrenal Venous Sampling in Primary Aldosteronism. Surgery. 2004. PMID: 15674694
Subject: Endocrinology

q: A 45-year-old physician with hypertension sees a pharmaceutical representative who offers $5,000 to speak at a dinner symposium about a new antihypertensive drug. The physician has been prescribing this medication and believes it's effective. His colleague suggests he shouldn't disclose this relationship to patients since he genuinely believes in the drug's efficacy. What is the most appropriate action?
A: Accept the speaking engagement without disclosure since his clinical judgment is independent
B: Decline the speaking engagement to avoid any potential conflicts
C: Accept the engagement and disclose the financial relationship to affected patients
D: Accept the engagement but only prescribe the drug to patients who specifically request it
c: C - Accept the engagement and disclose the financial relationship to affected patients
e: Physicians must disclose financial relationships with pharmaceutical or device companies that could reasonably be perceived as influencing clinical decisions, regardless of whether they believe their judgment is independent. This includes speaking fees, research funding, consulting agreements, and other compensation. Disclosure preserves patient autonomy by allowing informed decision-making and maintains trust in the physician-patient relationship. The physician's genuine belief in the medication's efficacy doesn't eliminate the potential conflict of interest. Professional integrity requires transparency about financial incentives that could influence prescribing patterns, even when the physician believes their clinical judgment remains objective. Patients have the right to know about potential conflicts when making healthcare decisions.
kp: Physicians must disclose financial relationships with industry that could influence clinical decisions to patients.
kp_cloze: Physicians must disclose [____] with industry that could influence clinical decisions to patients.
d: Accept the speaking engagement without disclosure since his clinical judgment is independent: The physician's genuine belief in the drug's efficacy might suggest disclosure is unnecessary, but financial relationships create potential conflicts of interest that must be disclosed regardless of perceived independence, as patients deserve transparency about factors that could influence prescribing decisions.

Decline the speaking engagement to avoid any potential conflicts: The colleague's suggestion and the complexity of managing conflicts might make complete avoidance seem appropriate, but physicians can ethically accept legitimate professional opportunities with proper disclosure rather than avoiding all industry relationships.

Accept the engagement but only prescribe the drug to patients who specifically request it: This approach might seem to eliminate conflict by making prescribing patient-driven, but it doesn't address the disclosure requirement and inappropriately shifts clinical decision-making responsibility to patients who lack medical expertise.
References: Lo B, Field MJ. Conflict of Interest in Medical Research, Education, and Practice. National Academies Press. 2009. PMID: 25032318
Chimonas S, Brennan TA, Rothman DJ. Physicians and Drug Representatives: Exploring the Dynamics of the Relationship. J Gen Intern Med. 2007. PMID: 17356988
Grande D, Frosch DL, Perkins AW, et al. Effect of Exposure to Small Pharmaceutical Promotional Items on Treatment Preferences. Arch Intern Med. 2009. PMID: 19822826
Subject: Medical Ethics

brainstorm.hy_clue: pharmaceutical speaking fee, colleague suggests no disclosure needed, genuine belief in drug efficacy, financial relationship with industry, prescribing same medication
brainstorm.action_content: disclose financial relationship to affected patients
brainstorm.dx_distractor: no conflict exists due to genuine belief + fails because financial relationships create potential conflicts regardless of perceived independence
brainstorm.action_distractor: avoid all industry relationships + fails because physicians can ethically accept opportunities with proper disclosure

phrase: Financial relationships with industry require patient disclosure regardless of physician belief

q: A 45-year-old agricultural worker presents to the ED with 2 hours of nausea, vomiting, and difficulty breathing after mixing pesticides. His wife mentions he seemed confused and was sweating profusely. Examination shows miosis, excessive salivation, muscle fasciculations, and wheezing. Heart rate is 52 bpm. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
e: This patient presents with classic organophosphate poisoning manifesting as cholinergic crisis with DUMBBELSS symptoms (Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm/Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating). Organophosphates irreversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase, causing excessive acetylcholine accumulation at synapses. Dual antidote therapy is required: atropine competitively blocks muscarinic receptors, titrated to dry secretions and reverse bronchospasm, while pralidoxime (2-PAM) reactivates acetylcholinesterase if given within 24-48 hours before permanent enzyme "aging" occurs. Atropine addresses life-threatening respiratory symptoms immediately, while pralidoxime prevents progression and aids recovery. Both medications work synergistically and should be administered together in confirmed organophosphate exposure with clinical toxicity.
kp: Organophosphate poisoning requires dual therapy: atropine for muscarinic symptoms plus pralidoxime to reactivate acetylcholinesterase.
kp_cloze: Organophosphate poisoning requires dual therapy: atropine for muscarinic symptoms plus [____] to reactivate acetylcholinesterase.

d.Activated charcoal and supportive care: The agricultural exposure and GI symptoms might suggest general toxic ingestion requiring decontamination, but the specific constellation of cholinergic signs (miosis, salivation, fasciculations, bradycardia) indicates organophosphate poisoning requiring specific antidotes, not just supportive measures.
d.Naloxone and thiamine: The altered mental status and respiratory depression could mimic opioid intoxication, but miosis with excessive secretions, fasciculations, and bradycardia are inconsistent with opioid toxidrome; organophosphate poisoning requires anticholinergic therapy, not opioid reversal.
d.Flumazenil and gastric lavage: The confusion and respiratory symptoms might suggest benzodiazepine overdose, but the hypersecretory state, miosis, and fasciculations are pathognomonic for cholinergic excess; flumazenil would not address acetylcholinesterase inhibition and could precipitate seizures.

brainstorm.action_content: atropine plus pralidoxime for cholinergic crisis
brainstorm.dx_distractor: opioid overdose - miosis suggests opioids but hypersecretion contradicts
brainstorm.action_distractor: naloxone alone - treats wrong receptor system, no cholinergic blockade

phrase: DUMBBELSS plus fasciculations equals atropine plus pralidoxime
References: Eddleston M, Buckley NA, Eyer P, et al. Management of acute organophosphorus pesticide poisoning. Lancet. 2008. PMID: 18222499
King AM, Aaron CK. Organophosphate and carbamate poisoning. Emerg Med Clin North Am. 2015. PMID: 25455664
Rotenberg JS, Newmark J. Nerve agent attacks on children: diagnosis and management. Pediatrics. 2003. PMID: 12949300
Subject: Emergency Medicine

q: A 68-year-old man presents with 4 days of right knee pain and swelling. He underwent total knee replacement 2 years ago and recently completed a dental procedure. Temperature is 100.8°F. Knee is warm and tender with decreased range of motion. His primary care physician wonders if this could be osteoarthritis flare given his age. Arthrocentesis yields cloudy synovial fluid with WBC 1,400/uL (normal <200), 85% neutrophils. Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
C: Urgent orthopedic consultation for surgical debridement
c: C - Urgent orthopedic consultation for surgical debridement
e: Prosthetic joint infection requires urgent surgical intervention due to biofilm formation on prosthetic materials that prevents antibiotic penetration. The lower synovial WBC threshold (≥1,100/uL vs ≥50,000 in native joints) reflects the immunocompromised environment around prostheses. Gram-positive cocci in clusters suggest Staphylococcus species, common in prosthetic infections. Recent dental work provides a likely source of bacteremia. Surgical options include debridement with prosthesis retention (if caught early with stable implant) or prosthesis exchange. IV antibiotics alone have poor success rates due to biofilm protection. The combination of surgical debridement plus prolonged antibiotics (typically 6 weeks IV followed by oral) provides the best outcomes for prosthetic joint infections.
kp: Prosthetic joint infection requires urgent surgical debridement plus antibiotics; synovial WBC ≥1,100 is diagnostic threshold.
kp_cloze: Prosthetic joint infection requires urgent surgical debridement plus antibiotics; synovial WBC [≥1,100] is diagnostic threshold.

d.Intra-articular corticosteroid injection: The physician's suggestion of osteoarthritis flare and the patient's age might suggest inflammatory arthritis requiring steroid injection, but the fever, elevated synovial WBC count, and positive gram stain indicate active infection where steroids would worsen outcomes by suppressing immune response.
d.Oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-u: The recent dental procedure and moderate symptoms might suggest a mild infection manageable with oral antibiotics, but prosthetic joint infections require biofilm disruption through surgery, as oral antibiotics cannot achieve adequate tissue penetration around prosthetic materials.
d.Intravenous antibiotics for 6 weeks: While IV antibiotics are part of prosthetic joint infection treatment and the positive gram stain confirms bacterial infection, antibiotics alone fail due to biofilm formation on prosthetic surfaces that prevents antibiotic penetration, requiring surgical debridement first.

brainstorm.action_content: urgent orthopedic consultation for surgical debridement
brainstorm.dx_distractor: osteoarthritis flare - fails due to fever and positive gram stain
brainstorm.action_distractor: IV antibiotics alone - fails due to biofilm requiring surgical disruption

phrase: Prosthetic joint: WBC ≥1,100 needs surgery plus antibiotics
References: Osmon DR, Berbari EF, Berendt AR, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Prosthetic Joint Infection. Clin Infect Dis. 2013. PMID: 23223583
Tande AJ, Patel R. Prosthetic Joint Infection. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2014. PMID: 24696436
Zimmerli W, Trampuz A, Ochsner PE. Prosthetic-Joint Infections. N Engl J Med. 2004. PMID: 15470219
Subject: Orthopedics

q: A 28-year-old sexually active man presents with sudden onset of severe left knee pain, swelling, and inability to bear weight for 2 days. He reports a mild URI 1 week ago treated with over-the-counter medications. Temperature 38.8°C, knee is warm, erythematous, and exquisitely tender with 40mL purulent fluid aspirated. Synovial fluid shows 85,000 WBC/uL (normal <200) with 92% neutrophils. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
e: Septic arthritis requires immediate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic coverage before culture results. The combination of vancomycin plus ceftriaxone covers the most common pathogens: vancomycin provides MRSA coverage (important given rising resistance), while ceftriaxone covers Neisseria gonorrhoeae (crucial in sexually active adults) and other gram-negative organisms. The synovial fluid WBC count >50,000 with neutrophil predominance strongly suggests bacterial infection. Young sexually active patients have increased risk of gonococcal arthritis, which can present as acute monoarticular arthritis. Treatment should never be delayed for culture results given the rapid joint destruction that can occur. Antibiotics are later adjusted based on culture and sensitivity results, typically after 48-72 hours.
kp: Septic arthritis requires empiric vancomycin plus ceftriaxone for MRSA and gonococcal coverage before culture results.
kp_cloze: Septic arthritis requires empiric [____] plus ceftriaxone for MRSA and gonococcal coverage before culture results.

d.Intravenous ceftriaxone alone: The patient's sexual activity and young age raise concern for gonococcal arthritis, which ceftriaxone covers appropriately, but monotherapy fails to provide MRSA coverage, which is essential given the high morbidity of untreated staphylococcal joint infections and rising community MRSA rates.
d.Oral ciprofloxacin and NSAIDs: The recent URI and joint symptoms might suggest a reactive arthritis that could respond to anti-inflammatory treatment, but the purulent aspirate with 85,000 WBCs and 92% neutrophils indicates acute bacterial infection requiring intravenous antibiotics, not oral therapy or anti-inflammatories.
d.High-dose corticosteroids and colchicine: The acute monoarticular presentation with significant inflammation could mimic crystal arthropathy (gout or pseudogout), but the fever, purulent fluid, and extremely high synovial WBC count with neutrophil predominance indicate bacterial infection where steroids would be contraindicated.

brainstorm.action_content: empiric vancomycin plus ceftriaxone before cultures
brainstorm.dx_distractor: crystal arthropathy - fever and purulent fluid rule out
brainstorm.action_distractor: ceftriaxone alone - misses MRSA coverage in septic arthritis

phrase: Septic joint: vancomycin plus ceftriaxone covers MRSA and gonorrhea
References: Ross JJ, Saltzman CL, Carling P, et al. Pneumococcal septic arthritis: review of 190 cases. Clin Infect Dis. 2003. PMID: 14562906
Mathews CJ, Weston VC, Jones A, et al. Bacterial septic arthritis in adults. Lancet. 2010. PMID: 20510461
García-Arias M, Balsa A, Mola EM. Septic arthritis. Best Pract Res Clin Rheumatol. 2011. PMID: 22137921
Subject: Internal Medicine

q: A 67-year-old man with atrial fibrillation presents for anticoagulation evaluation. He has hypertension, diabetes, and had a TIA 2 years ago. His cardiologist mentioned he might need a different medication than younger patients. Echocardiogram shows normal ejection fraction and mild mitral regurgitation. His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5. What is the most appropriate anticoagulation therapy?
e: This patient has non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 5 (age ≥65, hypertension, diabetes, prior stroke/TIA), indicating high stroke risk requiring anticoagulation. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) including apixaban are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular atrial fibrillation due to superior safety profile, fewer drug interactions, and no need for routine monitoring. The mild mitral regurgitation does not constitute valvular atrial fibrillation, which specifically refers to moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical prosthetic valves. Valvular atrial fibrillation requires warfarin as DOACs are contraindicated. The cardiologist's comment about different medication likely referred to age-appropriate dosing, but apixaban remains first-line for stroke prevention in this high-risk patient.
kp: Non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 requires DOAC as first-line anticoagulation over warfarin
kp_cloze: Non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 requires [____] as first-line anticoagulation over warfarin

d.Aspirin 325 mg daily: The high CHA2DS2-VASc score and history of TIA suggest need for anticoagulation rather than antiplatelet therapy, but aspirin alone is inadequate for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation with this level of risk and is not recommended in current guidelines.
d.Warfarin with INR goal 2-3: The cardiologist's comment about different medications for older patients might suggest warfarin as traditional therapy, but DOACs are now preferred first-line agents for non-valvular atrial fibrillation due to better safety and efficacy profile compared to warfarin.
d.Clopidogrel 75 mg daily: While this patient has diabetes which might suggest antiplatelet therapy, clopidogrel monotherapy is insufficient for stroke prevention in high-risk atrial fibrillation and anticoagulation is specifically indicated given the CHA2DS2-VASc score of 5.

References: January CT, Wann LS, Calkins H, et al. 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Focused Update on Atrial Fibrillation. Circulation. 2019. PMID: 30686041
Steffel J, Collins R, Antz M, et al. 2021 European Heart Rhythm Association Practical Guide on DOACs. Europace. 2021. PMID: 33895845
Lip GY, Banerjee A, Boriani G, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for Atrial Fibrillation: CHEST Guidelines. Chest. 2018. PMID: 30144419
Subject: Cardiology

brainstorm.hy_clue: 67yo male, AF, HTN/DM/prior TIA, CHA2DS2-VASc=5, mild MR
brainstorm.action_content: DOAC preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF
brainstorm.dx_distractor: Valvular AF requiring warfarin - fails because mild MR ≠ valvular AF
brainstorm.action_distractor: Warfarin first-line - fails because DOACs now preferred

q: A 45-year-old man presents with recurrent epigastric pain and watery diarrhea for 6 months. He has had three episodes of peptic ulcer disease despite triple therapy for H. pylori. His wife mentions he has gained weight recently due to increased appetite. Physical examination shows mild epigastric tenderness. Fasting gastrin level is 1,200 pg/mL (normal <100). What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
e: This patient presents with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma): multiple refractory ulcers, secretory diarrhea, and markedly elevated fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL. The secretin stimulation test is the confirmatory diagnostic test for gastrinoma. In normal individuals, secretin suppresses gastrin release, but in gastrinoma patients, secretin paradoxically stimulates gastrin secretion, typically increasing levels >120 pg/mL above baseline. This occurs because gastrinoma cells express secretin receptors that stimulate gastrin release, unlike normal G-cells. Once confirmed biochemically, imaging with CT or MRI localizes the tumor for surgical planning. The weight gain mentioned is a red herring suggesting increased appetite rather than the typical weight loss from malabsorption.
kp: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome confirmed by secretin stimulation test showing paradoxical gastrin increase.
kp_cloze: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome confirmed by [____] showing paradoxical gastrin increase.

d.Upper endoscopy with biopsy: The recurrent ulcers despite H. pylori treatment might suggest need for direct visualization and tissue sampling, but with gastrin >1000 pg/mL already establishing probable gastrinoma, functional confirmation with secretin stimulation test is the appropriate next step before anatomical localization.
d.24-hour urine gastrin measurement: The diarrhea and systemic symptoms could suggest measuring gastrin excretion over time for better assessment, but 24-hour urine gastrin is not a standard diagnostic test; serum gastrin levels and secretin stimulation testing are the established diagnostic approach.
d.CT abdomen with contrast: The markedly elevated gastrin suggests immediate need for tumor localization, but biochemical confirmation with secretin stimulation test should precede imaging to definitively establish the diagnosis before surgical planning.

brainstorm.action_content: secretin stimulation test for biochemical confirmation
brainstorm.dx_distractor: refractory H. pylori infection + fails because gastrin markedly elevated
brainstorm.action_distractor: immediate CT imaging + fails because biochemical confirmation needed first

phrase: Secretin paradoxically increases gastrin in gastrinoma patients
references: Ito T, Cadiot G, Jensen RT. Diagnosis of Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome: Increasingly Difficult. World J Gastroenterol. 2012. PMID: 22736902
Rehfeld JF, Friis-Hansen L, Goetze JP, Hansen TV. The Biology of Cholecystokinin and Gastrin Peptides. Curr Top Med Chem. 2007. PMID: 17305573
Norton JA, Fraker DL, Alexander HR, et al. Surgery to Cure the Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome. N Engl J Med. 1999. PMID: 10528035
subject: Gastroenterology

q: A 67-year-old woman undergoes routine mammography showing clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. She has a family history of colon cancer and takes hormone replacement therapy. Physical examination reveals no palpable masses. Core needle biopsy shows ductal carcinoma in situ with high-grade features and no invasion. What is the most appropriate treatment?
e: DCIS typically presents as clustered microcalcifications on mammography and is non-invasive by definition. Standard treatment is breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with clear margins followed by radiation therapy, which reduces local recurrence risk from 25-30% to 10-15%. The addition of tamoxifen may be considered for hormone receptor-positive DCIS but is not universally required. High-grade features increase recurrence risk but do not change the initial surgical approach. Bilateral mastectomy would be overtreatment for unilateral DCIS without high-risk features like extensive disease or strong family history of breast/ovarian cancer. The hormone replacement therapy and colon cancer history are not contraindications to breast-conserving therapy.
kp: DCIS with microcalcifications requires lumpectomy with radiation therapy for optimal local control.
kp_cloze: DCIS with microcalcifications requires [____] for optimal local control.
d: Bilateral mastectomy: The family history of colon cancer and hormone replacement therapy use might suggest increased cancer risk warranting aggressive surgical approach, but DCIS is non-invasive and unilateral disease without high-risk genetic factors does not justify bilateral mastectomy.
